refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and operational context. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, teleoperation overhead, and safety system energy draw. The refined FR establishes the performance envelope and measurement protocol as the functional requirement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption (kWh) and material moved (tons) over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 40-hour work week, representative task mix). Calculate energy_per_ton at regular intervals. Verify that measured values remain ≤ E_target. Decompose energy sources: propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation network, safety systems, auxiliary loads. Identify which subsystems dominate and which are controllable.
sub_frs: FR-EE-1: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit work output (propulsion, hydraulic actuation, auxiliary systems).
FR-EE-2: The teleoperation system shall minimize network and compute overhead energy relative to total system energy budget.
FR-EE-3: The safety services system shall execute safety monitoring and intervention with bounded energy overhead.
FR-EE-4: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time visibility into energy consumption by subsystem and cumulative energy_per_ton metric.
FR-EE-5: The operator (on-site or remote) shall be enabled to make energy-aware operational decisions (e.g., task sequencing, speed, load optimization).

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a closed-loop energy management system that monitors real-time power draw across all subsystems, calculates rolling energy_per_ton, and provides feedback to the operator; couple with vehicle efficiency optimization (e.g., engine tuning, hydraulic efficiency) and teleoperation network optimization (e.g., adaptive bitrate, edge compute).

DP-B: Establish a static energy budget allocation per subsystem (vehicle, teleoperation, safety) based on E_target; enforce hard limits via power management firmware; require operator training on energy-aware task planning.

DP-C: Implement predictive energy modeling based on task type, terrain, load, and operator behavior; use model to recommend task sequences and operational parameters that minimize energy_per_ton; integrate with teleoperation and vehicle control systems.

DP-D: Combine real-time energy monitoring (DP-A) with predictive modeling (DP-C) and adaptive subsystem control; enable dynamic reallocation of energy budget based on mission priority (safety > productivity > efficiency).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement an integrated energy management architecture comprising: (1) real-time energy telemetry from vehicle (propulsion, hydraulics, auxiliary), teleoperation (network, compute), and safety systems; (2) predictive energy model trained on task type, terrain, load, and operator behavior; (3) adaptive control logic that dynamically allocates energy budget across subsystems while maintaining safety as the non-negotiable constraint; (4) operator-facing dashboard displaying current energy_per_ton, subsystem breakdown, and predictive guidance for energy-efficient task execution; (5) closed-loop feedback from measured energy_per_ton to model refinement and control parameter tuning.

potential_sources: Energy monitoring (telemetry) is tightly coupled to vehicle control and teleoperation network; requires standardized sensor interfaces and data formats across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.
Predictive model depends on accurate task classification and operator intent; requires integration with Tasking interface and operator input.
Adaptive control logic must respect safety constraints enforced by Safety Services; requires SafetyAlert interface and coordination protocol.
Energy budget reallocation may conflict with real-time safety interventions; mitigation: safety always takes priority, energy optimization is secondary.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is moderately coupled but couplings are well-defined and manageable via explicit interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). The architecture avoids circular dependencies by establishing a clear priority hierarchy: Safety > Availability > Efficiency.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Accurate energy telemetry is available from all subsystems (vehicle, teleoperation, safety) with sufficient granularity and latency (< 1 second) to enable real-time feedback.
ASSUMPTION: Predictive energy model can be trained on historical data from similar equipment and worksites; model accuracy is sufficient (e.g., ±10%) to guide operator decisions.
ASSUMPTION: Operator (on-site or remote) is willing and able to act on energy efficiency guidance without compromising safety or productivity.

RISK: If energy telemetry is unavailable or unreliable, the system cannot measure energy_per_ton and cannot verify compliance with E_target. Mitigation: implement redundant sensors and fallback estimation methods.

RISK: If predictive model is inaccurate or biased, operator guidance may be misleading and lead to suboptimal or unsafe decisions. Mitigation: validate model offline before deployment; continuously refine model based on measured outcomes.

RISK: Adaptive control logic may introduce latency or instability if not carefully tuned. Mitigation: implement robust control design with safety margins; extensive simulation and field testing before deployment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly define: (1) energy telemetry data model and sampling rates; (2) predictive model training data requirements and validation criteria; (3) adaptive control algorithm and tuning parameters; (4) operator interface design and decision support logic; (5) fallback behavior if telemetry or model fails. These details should be captured in sub-level DPs during decomposition.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the energy management architecture (real-time monitoring, predictive modeling, adaptive control, operator dashboard) and coordinates energy budget allocation across subsystems while enforcing safety constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry from Vehicle (propulsion power, hydraulic pressure/flow, auxiliary loads), Teleoperation (network latency, compute load, bandwidth), and Safety Services (safety system power draw, intervention frequency). Used to calculate real-time energy_per_ton and train predictive model.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Inbound task definitions (task type, target load, terrain, duration estimate) from Site Manager or Tele-operator. Used to initialize predictive energy model and provide context for operator guidance.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface with Safety Services. Inbound: safety constraints and intervention signals. Outbound: energy budget reallocation requests (e.g., reduce non-critical loads to free energy for safety systems). Ensures safety is never compromised by energy optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with Tele-operator and Site Manager. Outbound: energy_per_ton dashboard, subsystem breakdown, predictive guidance. Inbound: operator commands and energy optimization preferences.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the energy management architecture. It integrates all subsystems and owns the cross-cutting energy coordination logic. Minimal diffusion: only the operator dashboard and feedback loop extend to external stakeholders (Site Manager, Tele-operator).

Vehicle owns propulsion and hydraulic efficiency optimization, which are core to energy_per_ton. However, Vehicle cannot own the cross-system energy coordination logic or the predictive model (which depends on teleoperation and safety overhead). Assigning energy management to Vehicle would create high diffusion and coupling.

Teleoperation owns network and compute efficiency, which contribute to energy_per_ton. However, Teleoperation cannot own the overall energy management architecture or vehicle efficiency optimization. Assigning energy management to Teleoperation would create high diffusion and coupling.

Safety Services owns safety system energy overhead but does not own the overall energy management architecture or cross-system coordination. Assigning energy management to Safety Services would violate the principle that safety is a constraint, not the primary objective.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of the energy management architecture because: (1) it is the top-level system boundary that integrates all subsystems; (2) it owns the cross-cutting energy coordination logic and predictive model; (3) it interfaces with external stakeholders (Site Manager, Tele-operator) for energy-aware decision support; (4) it enforces the priority hierarchy (Safety > Availability > Efficiency) across subsystems. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) own their respective efficiency optimizations but report telemetry to NGHE Solution for coordination.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution becomes a 'god module' that owns too much logic, it may become difficult to test, maintain, and evolve. Mitigation: decompose energy management into sub-modules (energy monitor, predictive model, adaptive controller, dashboard) during next level of decomposition; assign clear ownership and interfaces.

RISK: If energy telemetry is not standardized across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, NGHE Solution may struggle to integrate and correlate data. Mitigation: establish a common telemetry data model and interface specification; require all subsystems to conform.

RISK: If adaptive control logic is not carefully isolated from safety-critical logic, energy optimization may inadvertently compromise safety. Mitigation: implement clear separation of concerns; safety constraints are hard limits, energy optimization is a soft objective; extensive testing and formal verification of safety properties.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary for energy management. However, the scope should be refined to explicitly exclude subsystem-level efficiency optimizations (which belong to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and focus on cross-system coordination, monitoring, and operator decision support. Sub-level decomposition should define the internal module structure (energy monitor, predictive model, adaptive controller, dashboard) and their interfaces.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (integrated energy management architecture) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). The DP provides mechanisms for real-time monitoring, predictive guidance, and adaptive control that enable the system to measure and optimize energy efficiency. The DP is neither over-constrained nor under-constrained; it allows for multiple implementation approaches (e.g., different predictive models, control algorithms) while maintaining the core functional intent.

diffusion_risks: Energy telemetry must be collected from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; if telemetry interfaces are not standardized, NGHE Solution may need to implement multiple adapters, increasing diffusion.

Predictive model training requires historical data from multiple worksites and equipment; if data is not available or is proprietary to subsystems, NGHE Solution may struggle to build an accurate model.

Operator dashboard and decision support logic may need to be customized for different stakeholder roles (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Maintenance); if not carefully abstracted, this could increase diffusion.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner of the energy management architecture. The module boundary is clear: NGHE Solution owns the cross-system coordination logic, monitoring, and operator interface; subsystems own their respective efficiency optimizations and report telemetry via standardized interfaces. Diffusion is minimal and manageable via explicit interface definitions.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-EE (energy efficiency KPI) is feasible via measurement of energy_per_ton over the agreed evaluation period. The NGHE Solution module provides the infrastructure (telemetry collection, metric calculation, dashboard) to enable this measurement. Verification requires: (1) accurate energy sensors on Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) reliable data collection and aggregation; (3) clear definition of evaluation period and worksite scenarios; (4) acceptance criteria (E_target) agreed with customer.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The refined FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the top-level owner, with sub-FRs allocated to Vehicle (propulsion/hydraulic efficiency), Teleoperation (network/compute efficiency), Safety Services (safety system overhead), and NGHE Solution (monitoring, modeling, coordination, operator interface). Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify the energy telemetry data model, sampling rates, and interface specifications. Ensure all subsystems conform to a common standard.
Define the predictive energy model in more detail: input features (task type, terrain, load, operator behavior), training data requirements, validation criteria, and update frequency.
Specify the adaptive control algorithm: decision logic, tuning parameters, safety constraints, and fallback behavior if telemetry or model fails.
Design the operator dashboard and decision support interface: information architecture, visualization, actionable recommendations, and user testing plan.
Establish a measurement and verification plan for energy_per_ton: sensor selection, data collection protocol, evaluation period, worksite scenarios, and acceptance criteria.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined and the selected DP is sound, but the DP is not yet sufficiently detailed to guide implementation. The DP-D architecture requires decomposition into sub-level DPs for: (1) energy telemetry collection and standardization; (2) predictive energy modeling; (3) adaptive control logic; (4) operator dashboard and decision support; (5) measurement and verification. These sub-level DPs will refine the design and enable allocation to specific implementation modules (e.g., software components, hardware sensors, network protocols). The NGHE Solution IM boundary is appropriate, but sub-level decomposition will define the internal module structure and interfaces.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-1.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall minimize energy consumption per unit work output through efficient propulsion, hydraulic actuation, and auxiliary system design.
FR-EE-2.1: The Teleoperation subsystem shall minimize network and compute overhead energy relative to total system energy budget.
FR-EE-3.1: The Safety Services subsystem shall execute safety monitoring and intervention with bounded energy overhead.
FR-EE-4.1: The NGHE Solution shall collect real-time energy telemetry from all subsystems via standardized interfaces.
FR-EE-4.2: The NGHE Solution shall calculate and display real-time energy_per_ton metric and subsystem energy breakdown.
FR-EE-5.1: The NGHE Solution shall provide predictive energy guidance to operators based on task type, terrain, load, and historical data.
FR-EE-5.2: The NGHE Solution shall enable operators to make energy-aware operational decisions (e.g., task sequencing, speed, load optimization) without compromising safety or productivity.

assumptions: Accurate energy telemetry is available from all subsystems with sufficient granularity and latency (< 1 second).
Predictive energy model can be trained on historical data with accuracy ±10%.
Operator is willing and able to act on energy efficiency guidance.
Safety is the non-negotiable constraint; energy optimization is secondary.
Customer-defined E_target is achievable with the selected DP architecture.
Evaluation period and worksite scenarios are representative of operational use cases.
open_risks: Energy telemetry may be unavailable or unreliable; mitigation: implement redundant sensors and fallback estimation.
Predictive model may be inaccurate or biased; mitigation: validate offline, continuously refine based on measured outcomes.
Adaptive control logic may introduce latency or instability; mitigation: robust control design, extensive testing.
Operator may not trust or act on energy guidance; mitigation: user research, iterative dashboard design, operator training.
Energy optimization may conflict with other objectives (e.g., safety, productivity); mitigation: clear priority hierarchy, explicit trade-off analysis.
evidence_needed: Energy telemetry specifications and sensor selection for Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services.
Predictive energy model design: features, training data, validation criteria, update frequency.
Adaptive control algorithm design: decision logic, tuning parameters, safety constraints, fallback behavior.
Operator dashboard design: information architecture, visualization, user testing results.
Measurement and verification plan: sensor selection, data collection protocol, evaluation period, acceptance criteria.
Field trial data: measured energy_per_ton over representative worksite scenarios, comparison to E_target.
